does: This paper empirically evaluates refusal behavior in small, on-premises large language models when applied to legal tasks. The authors systematically test how different contextual prompts—particularly authority-style prefixes that establish legal roles such as acting as a supreme court assistant or defense lawyer—affect whether these models refuse to respond to legal queries. They measure refusal rates across several modern small LLMs baseline against various prompt framings and find that such authority prefixes dramatically increase refusal rates by 2–20× compared to unprompted baselines, while role-play jailbreak prefixes produce inconsistent results across models. The core contribution is demonstrating that these models exhibit unstable and context-dependent refusal patterns that could introduce systematic biases in legal assistance workflows.
connects: This work builds on growing research examining refusal behaviors and alignment in language models, extending prior investigations into how models handle sensitive domains like law and ethics. It connects to literature on prompt injection vulnerabilities, jailbreaking techniques, and the tension between over-refusal and helpfulness in safety-tuned models. The paper also engages with broader concerns about bias in AI systems applied to high-stakes domains and responds to the practical reality that legal professionals are already deploying small on-device LLMs despite unresolved safety questions. This work contributes to understanding how institutional and contextual framing can interact with model safety mechanisms in unexpected ways, a concern previously identified in studies of adversarial prompting and societal deployments of LLMs.
enables: This work highlights urgent follow-up investigations into the robustness of safety mechanisms in small deployable models under realistic use conditions. It opens questions about whether current alignment techniques generalize across different prompt contexts and whether models should be specialized or robustified differently for high-stakes domains. The findings suggest the need for better testing frameworks and deployment guidelines for on-premises legal AI assistants, as well as research into whether refusal behaviors can be made more stable and predictable. More broadly, it enables work on understanding how institutional context shapes model behavior and points toward developing more reliable, bias-aware AI systems for professional domains where selective refusal could have concrete consequences for access to legal assistance.
